name: Ruy Lopez
description: One of the oldest and most respected openings. White develops the bishop to b5, pinning the knight.

moves.commentary: Central pawn advance. This move controls the center and opens lines for your queen and bishop.

children.commentary: Black mirrors with e5, establishing a classical pawn center.
Nf3 develops the knight with tempo, attacking e5 and preparing to castle kingside.
Nc6 defends e5 and develops. This leads to the Ruy Lopez proper.
Bb5 pins the knight. This is the defining move of the Ruy Lopez.
The Morphy Defense. Black asks the bishop what it wants to do.
Ba4 keeps the bishop active while retreating from the pawn.
Nf6 develops and prepares to castle.
Castling kingside brings the king to safety and connects the rooks.
Be7 completes development. The mainline Ruy Lopez position.
The Berlin Defense. Black develops the knight instead of playing a6.
The Steinitz Defense. Black supports e5 with the pawn.
The Classical Defense. Black develops the bishop to an active square.

defenses.name: Berlin Defense
defenses.profile: The Berlin Defense is a solid response that avoids the main Ruy Lopez theory.

defenses.name: Marshall Attack
defenses.profile: The Marshall Attack is a sharp counterattacking system.

strategy.keyIdea: Pressure the knight on c6 that defends e5, indirectly threatening to win the central pawn.
strategy.middleGamePlan: Castle kingside and play d3 or d4 to control the center. The Ruy Lopez is a long-term game — White builds pressure slowly through piece activity and pawn tension rather than immediate tactics. Rook lifts and piece transfers to the kingside are common plans.
strategy.watchOut: Chasing the bishop retreat too eagerly — the bishop on b5/a4 is doing positional work and shouldn't be traded lightly.
typicalGoals: Maintain tension with the bishop on a4 as long as useful
Castle and prepare d4 to fight for the center
Use the c3-d4 pawn chain to gain central space
Look for piece transfers to the kingside in the later middlegame

quiz.question: Your bishop is on a4 putting pressure on the c6 knight. Your opponent plays ...b5 to chase it. What's the right idea?

options.text: Trade the bishop for the c6 knight immediately
options.explanation: Trading too early gives Black an easy game — the bishop has long-term value pressuring the position.

options.text: Retreat the bishop to b3 to maintain the diagonal
options.explanation: Correct. Bb3 keeps the bishop active on the a2-g8 diagonal while maintaining pressure. This is the standard Ruy Lopez response.

options.text: Ignore it and launch a kingside attack immediately
options.explanation: You haven't castled yet — attacking before completing development is premature in the Ruy Lopez.

default.opponentMoveSummary: Your opponent stepped off the main theory line with an unusual move, choosing their own path rather than the expected response.
default.whyBookBreaks: The standard line assumes a particular pawn or piece configuration that this move bypasses.
default.opponentStrategy: Your opponent is trying to reach a less-charted position where their preparation or intuition gives them an edge.
forwardGuidance: Prioritize completing your development — get all minor pieces off the back rank.
Control the center with pawns or piece pressure before launching any flank operations.

Nf6.opponentMoveSummary: Black played Nf6, developing the knight to f6 instead of playing the Morphy Defense with a6.
Nf6.whyBookBreaks: The Ruy Lopez main line assumes Black will play a6 (Morphy Defense) to challenge the bishop. Nf6 sidesteps that entirely.
Nf6.opponentStrategy: Berlin Defense — Black forces a knight trade and aims for a solid Berlin endgame that defuses White's attacking chances.
forwardGuidance: Expect exchanges; the Berlin often leads to an endgame where technique matters more than attack.
Develop solidly and control key squares — avoid overextending in the opening.

Bc5.opponentMoveSummary: Black played Bc5, developing the bishop to an active square and attacking your bishop on b5.
Bc5.whyBookBreaks: The main line expects a6. Bc5 launches an immediate counterattack on the bishop and changes the character of the position.
Bc5.opponentStrategy: Classical Defense — Black develops actively and challenges White's bishop before completing development.
forwardGuidance: Consider c3 and d4 to fight for the center; Black's bishop can become a target.
Complete development before committing — the position can become sharp.

d6.opponentMoveSummary: Black played d6, supporting e5 and preparing a solid but cramped development.
d6.whyBookBreaks: The Morphy Defense (a6) is the main response. d6 (Steinitz Defense) chooses solidity over flexibility.
d6.opponentStrategy: Steinitz Defense — Black accepts a cramped position in exchange for a solid pawn structure and slow development.
forwardGuidance: Play d4 when ready to open the center — Black's structure can become passive.
Develop pieces actively; the d6-e5 chain may become a target for piece pressure.
